segments.title: Grey Goose survives as a houseboat
segments.summary: Grey Goose is the surviving Steam Gun Boat, preserved in altered form after a postwar conversion to a houseboat in Kent.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens with the unusual survival story of Grey Goose, a Steam Gun Boat built by J. Samuel White at Cowes. He explains that she was sold around 1957, converted to a houseboat, and is now moored at Hoo St Werburgh in Medway, Kent. The moment also makes clear why restoration matters: she is a rare surviving example of this wartime coastal-force type.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Grey Goose, describes its postwar houseboat conversion, and gives its Medway location.

segments.title: A small remnant of a larger programme
segments.summary: Grey Fox's postwar fate introduces a Royal Navy Steam Gun Boat programme that produced seven completed boats and lost one.
segments.body: Grey Fox, unlike Grey Goose, was offered for sale in October 1947 and appears to have been scrapped. Dr. Clarke then sets the scale of the wartime effort: the Royal Navy had ordered many more boats than the surviving examples suggest, with seven completed and one lost. That contrast helps explain why the type is easy to overlook despite its intended role in a much larger coastal force.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Grey Fox, seven completed boats, and one loss.

segments.title: Steam Gun Boats as the costly coastal option
segments.summary: The Royal Navy paired Fairmiles with larger, costlier Steam Gun Boats in its response to the E-boat threat.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes two complementary British coastal-force responses: the Fairmiles and the larger Steam Gun Boats. The latter were more expensive but viable, and their planned prefabrication was meant to make large-scale production possible. Although the boats were not adopted in the hoped-for numbers, those that served performed well; he presents them as the closest Second World War example of a torpedo-boat destroyer.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Fairmiles and Steam Gun Boats, explains the planned prefabrication, and assesses their service record.
